intro:
Amos (Hebrew Amos, "burden" or "borne") was a herdsman of Tekoa in the hills of Judah whom God called to prophesy against the northern kingdom of Israel in the prosperous reign of Jeroboam II, about 760 BC. He is generally reckoned the earliest of the "writing prophets," and his book stands as the classic biblical indictment of social injustice cloaked in religious respectability.

Amos was no professional. When challenged, he protested, "I was no prophet, nor a prophet's son, but I was a herdsman and a dresser of sycamore figs. But the LORD took me from following the flock, and the LORD said to me, 'Go, prophesy to my people Israel'" (Amos 7:14–15). His call rested on sheer divine compulsion: "The lion has roared; who will not fear? The Lord GOD has spoken; who can but prophesy?" (Amos 3:8). Coming from Judah into the rival kingdom, he confronted a society outwardly thriving but rotten with greed and oppression.

His book opens with a series of oracles against the surrounding nations — Damascus, Gaza, Tyre, Edom, Ammon, Moab — for their atrocities, then turns the same logic devastatingly against Judah and finally Israel itself (Amos 2:6). Amos thunders against those who "sell the righteous for silver, and the needy for a pair of sandals," who "trample the head of the poor into the dust" while reclining in luxury. He rejects worship divorced from justice in God's own voice: "I hate, I despise your feasts... But let justice roll down like waters, and righteousness like an ever-flowing stream" (Amos 5:24). He overturned the popular hope of "the day of the LORD," warning that for the unrepentant it would be "darkness, and not light" (Amos 5:18).

His message was conveyed through striking visions — the locusts, the consuming fire, the plumb line measuring Israel for judgment (Amos 7:7–8), and the basket of summer fruit signifying that the end was ripe (Amos 8:1–2). For such preaching the priest Amaziah of Bethel accused him of conspiracy and ordered him to flee back to Judah (Amos 7:12–13). Yet the book does not end in unrelieved doom: it closes with the promise to "raise up the booth of David that is fallen" and restore the fortunes of God's people (Amos 9:11) — words James cites at the Jerusalem Council as fulfilled in the ingathering of the Gentiles (Acts 15:16–17). Amos remains the enduring witness that God requires righteousness and justice, not ritual alone, and that He defends the poor against their oppressors.
